items.question: If I ___ harder, I would have passed the exam.
explanations: 'Studied' (past simple) is the second conditional; the third needs the past perfect.
Correct! The if-clause uses the past perfect: 'If I had studied'.
'Study' is present simple, but this imagines the past.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: If you had asked, I ___ you.
explanations: 'Would help' is the second conditional; the third needs 'would have + participle'.
Correct! The result uses 'would have + participle': 'I would have helped you'.
'Helped' (past simple) doesn't express the imagined result.
'Will help' is a real future, not an imagined past.

items.question: If they had left earlier, they ___ the flight.
explanations: 'Wouldn't miss' is the second conditional; the third needs 'would have'.
Correct! The imagined past result uses 'wouldn't have missed'.
'Didn't miss' is a plain past fact, not the imagined result.
'Won't miss' is a real future, not an imagined past.

items.question: If she ___ the message, she'd have come.
explanations: 'Saw' (past simple) is the second conditional; the third needs the past perfect.
Correct! The if-clause uses the past perfect: 'If she had seen the message'.
'Sees' is present simple, but this imagines the past.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: If I had known, I ___ you.
explanations: Correct! 'Would have + participle': 'I would have told you' (tell → told).
'Telled' isn't a word — the participle is 'told'.
'Would told' is missing 'have'.
'Will tell' is a real future, not an imagined past.

items.question: Correct this: 'If I studied, I would have passed.'
options: If I had studied, I would have passed.
If I study, I would have passed.
If I had studied, I would passed.
If I would have studied, I passed.
explanations: Correct! The third conditional is all past: 'If I had studied, I would have passed'.
'If I study' is present simple, mixing tenses incorrectly.
'I would passed' is missing 'have'.
The if-clause never uses 'would have'.

items.question: If we had trained harder, we ___ the match.
explanations: 'Would win' is the second conditional; the third needs 'have + participle'.
Correct! 'Could have' can replace 'would have': 'we could have won'.
'Win' is present simple, but this imagines the past.
'Will win' is a real future, not an imagined past.

items.question: If I ___ to bed earlier, I would have felt better this morning.
explanations: 'Went' (past simple) is the second conditional; the third needs the past perfect.
Correct! The if-clause uses the past perfect: 'If I had gone to bed earlier'.
'Go' is present simple, but this imagines the past.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'I didn't know, so I didn't call you.'
options: If I had known, I would have called you.
If I knew, I'd call you.
If I know, I'll call you.
I call you if I know.
explanations: Correct! An imagined past takes the third conditional: 'If I had known, I would have called you'.
'If I knew, I'd call' is the second conditional, about now.
'If I know, I'll call' is the first conditional, a real future.
'I call you if I know' is a zero conditional (a habit).

items.question: If I ___ more time, I would have finished the essay.
explanations: A single 'had' is the auxiliary; you also need the participle of 'have'.
Correct! 'Had had' = auxiliary 'had' + participle 'had': 'If I had had more time'.
'Have had' is present perfect, but this imagines the past.
The if-clause never uses 'would have'.
